title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: This PR ships two new endpoints but has several blocking issues: (1) a module-level `refundsStore` singleton breaks test isolation and cannot be cleared between test runs; (2) the sort comparator sorts on the non-existent field `created_at` instead of the domain field `createdAt`, silently breaking the "newest-first" guarantee; (3) the history response includes a `card_number` field constructed via a suspicious `unknown` cast that tries to surface a full PAN – a clear security red flag; (4) there is no cumulative refund check, so a transaction can be refunded multiple times; (5) a "dollar-to-cents" heuristic in the refund amount guard silently produces wrong refund amounts. Beyond those blockers there are a cluster of should-fix issues: `console.log` bypassing the structured pino logger (which carries field-level redaction), a 200 response for a not-found transaction instead of a 404, the DI wiring in `createApp` being broken when `deps.payments` is injected, inconsistent camelCase/snake_case across the API surface, and tests so weak they would pass even if the feature were completely non-functional.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore shared across all instances and test runs
findings.message: `refundsStore` is declared at module scope (`const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}`), so every `RefundsService` instance writes into the same object for the lifetime of the Node.js process. This makes test runs non-deterministic: refunds created in one test bleed into every subsequent test in the same Jest worker. The store also cannot be reset between integration-test cases that each call `createApp()`. Fix: move the store inside the class as a private instance field (`private readonly store: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}`) so each service instance is fully isolated.

findings.title: Sort comparator reads `created_at` but Transaction domain field is `createdAt`
findings.message: The comparator accesses `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but the `Transaction` interface (domain/transaction.ts line 13) defines the field as `createdAt` (camelCase). Both values will always be `undefined`, making the sort a no-op — the "newest-first" ordering guarantee is silently broken for every response. Fix: change the comparator to use `a.createdAt` and `b.createdAt`, and remove the `any` casts so the compiler catches this class of mistake next time.

findings.title: Response includes `card_number` field that attempts to surface a full PAN
findings.message: Line 43 casts the transaction through `unknown` to access a `card_number` property and emits it as `card_number` in the JSON response. The `Transaction` type does not store the full card number (only `cardLast4`), so today the fallback to `tx.cardLast4` fires every time — but this code is clearly written in anticipation of (or in response to) a domain object that carries the full PAN. If `card_number` is ever added to `Transaction`, this will silently start leaking full PANs to every consumer of the history endpoint. The response also now has two card fields (`card_number` and `card_last4`) where only one is meaningful. Fix: remove the `card_number` field entirely; `card_last4` is sufficient and safe.

findings.title: Dollar-to-cents heuristic silently produces wrong refund amounts
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount`, the code guesses whether the caller sent dollars by multiplying by 100 and checking again. This produces silent wrong results: e.g. a `tx.amount = 5000` (50 USD in cents) with `refundAmount = 100` (1 USD in dollars) passes the first guard (100 < 5000) and is stored as a 100-cent refund instead of the intended 5000 cents. The entire branch is a symptom of an underspecified API contract. Fix: remove the heuristic, define the unit in the schema docs, and return a 400 validation error if the requested amount exceeds the transaction amount.

findings.title: No cumulative refund check — same transaction can be over-refunded
findings.message: The refund amount guard (`refundAmount > tx.amount`) compares only the *current* refund request against the original charge. It does not account for refunds that have already been issued. A caller can POST `/refunds` twice for the same transaction and receive twice the original amount back. Fix: before creating the refund, sum `listRefundsForTransaction(transactionId)` and reject if `existing_total + refundAmount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: DI wiring broken when `deps.payments` is injected — refunds see a different repo
findings.message: A new `transactionRepo` is always created on line 22, and it is always passed to `RefundsService` and `CustomerHistoryController`. If a caller supplies `deps.payments` (a `PaymentsService` that was constructed with its own repository), transactions charged through that service are invisible to the refunds and history controllers because they use the freshly-allocated, empty repo. Fix: either (a) expand `AppDeps` to allow injecting the repo directly and share it, or (b) thread the repo through `AppDeps` so the injected `PaymentsService` and the new services all operate on the same store.

findings.title: `console.log` bypasses the structured pino logger and its PII redaction
findings.message: The codebase uses a purpose-built pino logger (`src/logger.ts`) configured with field-level redaction for `card.number`, `card.cvc`, and related paths. Three `console.log` calls in `refundsService.ts` (lines 25 and 55) and one in `customerHistoryController.ts` (line 15) bypass this entirely — no request IDs, no log-level gating in tests, and no redaction. If sensitive data (e.g. a transaction ID that correlates to a card) is ever included in a log message, it will appear in plain text. Replace all three with `logger.info(...)` / `logger.warn(...)` using structured object arguments rather than string concatenation.

findings.title: Returns 200 with empty body when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: When `this.refunds.refund()` returns `null` (transaction not found), the controller responds with `200 OK` and `{}`. Callers cannot distinguish a successful refund from a silent no-op. The `notFound()` error factory already exists in `src/errors.ts` for exactly this purpose. Fix: `if (result == null) { next(notFound('Transaction not found')); return; }` — the error handler will produce the correct 404 response.

findings.title: History response uses snake_case while the rest of the API uses camelCase
findings.message: The existing `/charge` and `/transactions/:id` endpoints return the `Transaction` object verbatim in camelCase (`cardLast4`, `customerId`, `createdAt`, `gatewayReference`). The new history response explicitly remaps every field to snake_case (`card_last4`, `customer_id`, `created_at`, `gateway_reference`). The refund response in `refundsController.ts` also uses snake_case (`refund_id`, `transaction_id`). This inconsistency will force API consumers to handle two different field-naming conventions. Choose one convention (camelCase matches existing contracts) and apply it consistently.

findings.title: Refund `amount` accepts zero and negative values
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` allows callers to submit `0` or `-500`. The chargeSchema pattern uses `z.number().int().positive()` for the same semantic field. A zero-amount refund would pass validation and create a `RefundResult` with `amount: 0`, silently wasting an idempotency slot. Fix: `z.number().int().positive().optional()`.

findings.title: Tests have no meaningful assertions — they would pass for a completely broken implementation
findings.message: All three tests assert only that a value is defined or that the status code is below 500. They do not check: (1) that `POST /refunds` returns 201 with a `refund_id` and correct `amount`; (2) that a missing transaction returns 404 (not 200); (3) that `GET /customers/:id/transactions` returns 200, the correct `customer_id`, and a well-formed `transactions` array. The existing `chargeRoute.test.ts` shows the expected pattern (status codes, `toMatchObject`, field-level assertions). The new tests should follow the same standard.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` used for string comparison
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` should be `tx.customerId === customerId`. `customerId` is always a `string` (from `req.params`) and `tx.customerId` is `string | undefined`; loose equality offers no benefit here and silently suppresses type-checker feedback.

findings.title: `historyQuerySchema` is dead code — declared but never imported or used
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but never imported anywhere in the codebase. The history controller reads `req.params.id` directly without using it. Either wire it up to validate the route parameter or remove it.

findings.title: `reason` is accepted by the schema and API but silently discarded
findings.message: The refund schema exposes a `reason` field, the controller passes it to `RefundsService.refund()`, but the service parameter is named `_reason` and the value is never stored in `RefundResult` or used in any log. Either store `reason` in the refund record (useful for audit trails) or remove it from the schema to avoid misleading callers into thinking it is persisted.
